name: Ekur
description: Ekur, meaning "Mountain House" or "House of the Mountain", was the central temple and arguably the most important religious building in ancient Mesopotamia. Located in Nippur, it was dedicated to Enlil, the god of wind, storms, and kingship, and was considered the dwelling place of the gods on Earth. The Ekur was not merely a temple; it was believed to be the cosmic axis mundi, the point of connection between the heavens and the earth. Its construction was attributed to the Anunnaki gods themselves, and its architectural design reflected the Mesopotamian understanding of the cosmos. The temple complex served as a vital center for religious rituals, administrative functions, and the distribution of resources. It played a significant role in legitimizing royal power, as kings sought Enlil's blessing to rule. Over centuries, the Ekur was rebuilt and renovated by various Mesopotamian rulers, each adding to its grandeur and importance. The temple's prominence is evident in numerous Sumerian myths and hymns, which describe its splendor, its divine inhabitants, and the rituals performed within its sacred walls. The Ekur represented not only a physical structure but also the spiritual and political heart of Mesopotamian civilization. Its destruction or desecration was considered a catastrophic event, symbolizing the loss of divine favor and the collapse of the social order. It was a symbol of power and stability.
subtitle: House of the Mountain, Enlil's Abode

keyMyths.title: Enlil and Ninlil
keyMyths.description: This myth describes the courtship and marriage of Enlil and Ninlil, which is closely associated with Nippur and the Ekur. The story explains the birth of important deities and reinforces the sacredness of the temple.

keyMyths.title: Enlil Chooses the King
keyMyths.description: Sumerian kings sought legitimacy through Enlil's blessing, often received at the Ekur. This mythic association reinforced the king's divine right to rule and the Ekur's role in political power.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
sources.work: The Sumerians: Their History, Culture, and Character
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Sumerian civilization and religion.
